intitule: Lesson 14: Expressing Future Plans - Resource Exercise (Grammar & Vocabulary)

enonce.texte:
Read the sentences below. Identify the use of will and be going to. Then, complete the rules and the table.

SENTENCE A: “After I graduate, I am going to study journalism at the University of Yaoundé.”
SENTENCE B: “I promise I will call my parents every week when I am away.”
SENTENCE C: “Based on his good grades, he is going to be a great doctor.”
SENTENCE D: “The government will build more vocational training centers next year.”

questions.texte: Classify each sentence (A, B, C, D) into the correct category: 'Planned Intention / Prediction based on evidence' or 'Promise / Prediction without evidence / Future fact'.
questions.reponse: A: Planned Intention; B: Promise; C: Prediction based on evidence; D: Prediction without evidence (Future fact).

questions.texte: Complete the grammar rule with the correct form: "We use be going to + infinitive to talk about ______. We use will + infinitive to talk about ______."
questions.reponse: We use be going to + infinitive to talk about pre-planned intentions or predictions based on present evidence. We use will + infinitive to talk about spontaneous decisions, promises, offers, or predictions without evidence.

questions.texte: Transform these planned intentions into complete sentences using 'be going to'.

sousQuestions.texte: Marie / apply / for a scholarship to study abroad.
sousQuestions.reponse: Marie is going to apply for a scholarship to study abroad.

sousQuestions.texte: We / not / choose / a career only for money.
sousQuestions.reponse: We are not going to choose a career only for money.

sousQuestions.texte: He / persuade / his parents to let him become an artist.
sousQuestions.reponse: He is going to persuade his parents to let him become an artist.

questions.texte: Write a spontaneous promise or decision using 'will' for each situation.

sousQuestions.texte: Your friend is nervous about a job interview. (Promise to help them prepare)
sousQuestions.reponse: Don't worry. I will help you prepare for your interview.

sousQuestions.texte: You have just decided to become a volunteer teacher. (Announce your decision)
sousQuestions.reponse: I've made a decision. I will become a volunteer teacher.

intitule: Lesson 14: Expressing Future Plans - Competence Exercise (Writing a Persuasive Letter)

enonce.texte:
Context: You are a Cameroonian student in Terminale A. For the past two years, you have dreamed of becoming a journalist to expose social injustices (e.g., corruption, lack of schools). However, your parents want you to study law or medicine because they believe journalism has few job opportunities. They have even threatened to stop paying for your education if you choose journalism.

Task: Write a formal letter to your parents (or one of them). In this letter, you must:

  1. Explain your future plan (intention) using be going to.
  2. Make promises about your studies and success using will.
  3. Make a prediction about your future career and its positive impact on society using will.
  4. Persuade them to support your choice.

Vocabulary to use: profession, journalist, opportunities, society, convince, passion, responsible, success.

questions.texte: Write the letter. (Approximately 200-250 words). Follow the structure of a formal letter (Your address, date, salutation, body, closing).

sousQuestions.texte: Identify and underline two sentences where you used 'be going to' to express a planned intention.
sousQuestions.reponse: Student's own answer. Example: 'I am going to enrol in the journalism programme at the University of Buea.'

sousQuestions.texte: Identify and circle two sentences where you used 'will' to make a promise or a prediction.
sousQuestions.reponse: Student's own answer. Example: 'I promise I will work twice as hard as any law student.'

intitule: Lesson 14: Expressing Future Plans - Competence Exercise (Role-Play & Justification)

enonce.texte: Scenario: You are participating in a career fair at your lycée. You meet a successful entrepreneur who tells you, "You are still young. You will change your mind about your career path many times." Disagree politely and explain your firm plans using the future tenses.

questions.texte: Write a dialogue (8-10 lines) between you and the entrepreneur. Use at least three different structures from the lesson: be going to (planned intention), will (promise/prediction), and present continuous for future arrangements (e.g., 'I am meeting a mentor next week').

sousQuestions.texte: Justify your choice of tense for each future action in your dialogue. Explain why you used 'will' instead of 'going to' (or vice versa).
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'In line 3, I said "I am going to start an internship" because this is a pre-planned intention. In line 5, I said "I will prove you wrong" because it is a spontaneous promise/ prediction based on my confidence, not evidence.'
